headlines.title: TEAMSTER HELD ON CHARGE OF MURDER
headlines.subtitle: ALLEGES 'FRAMEUP'
headlines.fullText: J. C. Thompson, teamster, held on a murder charge In conenction with the Frisco wreck near Trinity Park Sunday morning when three men were killed, said Wednesday morning to "someone" had framed-up" the charge against him. He didn't cure any person in particular. are was working on a clew which he believed would prove he this centertien. The only evidence they have against him is that it was his horse on the bridge," he said. If it Proven that the horse only wandered onto the bridge, it is said the company's liability would not be lessened, because the cattle guards are supposed to be constructed to prevent such situations.

headlines.title: STRIKERSFORZIDDEN TO USE EPITHETS IN AFRICAN TRADE WAR
headlines.fullText: By Assorintel Press, CAPETOWN, Union of South Africa, Jan. 14 .- Proclamation of martial law throughout the Union of South Africa in response to the declaration of a general strike by the Federation of Trades, is regarded as making the issue a clear one between the state and the syndicatists, who have adopted a system of rapidly organized sympathetic atrikes. The origin of the dispute, In which the railroad men's protest against the their policy of retrenchment introduced by employers has been lost sight of which has brought the trade and commerce Destin of the character colony of to a the standati regulations which came into .fr. hatons mention of martial law. created sur- prier. From last evening the use of such epithets as "scab" and "blackleg" was prohibited under penalty of severe punishment, while any attempt to in- Umidate workers was forbidden. News- papers are debarred from publishing reporta calculated to promote ill will between the disputants. The strike region is divided into nine areas, each controlled by an officer vested with absolute power as in time of war.

headlines.title: FINANCIERS TO DISCUSS REGIONAL BANK PLANS
headlines.subtitle: Meeting Will Be Held Thursday to Consider Texas' Claims.
headlines.fullText: Texas financiers are expected to meet in the Bankers' Institute quarters over the Traders National Bank morning at 11 o'clock to consider me for lasing the emmmmsur ere regional bank before the reserve bank board when It meets in Houston Feb. 9 and 10. Three delegates have been asked from each of the reserve cities in The state. These cities are Fort Worth, Dallas, Waco, San Antonio, Houston and Gal- worth it be w partner smith and R. D. Gage. Up to Wednes- Smith Rt. headlines.title: ERUPTION ABATING; DEATHS IN JAPAN CAN'T BE ESTIMATED
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. TOKIO, Japan, Jan. 14 .- Official reports this afternoon from Southern Japan say the eruption of the volcano of Sakura-Jima is abating. The loss of life cannot yet be estimated. Salcura, where the greatest loss of life, undoubtedly occurred. can not be visited because the eruption of Sakura-Jima continues. Sakura, Kagoshima, the nearest big city to it suffered great damage from the earthquakes, does not appear to have sustained a severe loss of life. H. Ijuin, former Japanese minister to Chinn, who lives at Kagoshima, reported to the foregin office that ten persons have been killed and thirty Injured here. Soldiers are camped in the squares of the city, while the population has sougth refuge in shrines and temples in the hills, The American consul at Nagasaki, Carl Eichel, reported as follows: A private telegram states that the Americans in Kagoshima fled to Sendai, near Kagoshima. All safe." missionaries were stationed The police and soldiers of Kagoshima soon after the disturbance began watching the door of Elliott's and of Sakura. Thousands of the city- kneit on the beach Batt-submerged in water and gesticulated wildly to the steamers and fehler boats for aid. One small steamer saved 300 persons, while other boats also did noble rescue work. The Japanese destroyers that were dispatched to the scene were unable to complete the journey because of the stormy weather and high seas. A squad champagne atmosphere interferes with wireless reports.

headlines.title: GREEK-TURK WAR FEARED BY GREECE, APPEAL TO EUROPE
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press, BERNE, Switzerland, Jan. 14 .- Assistance of the great powers in the prevention of another war between Greece and Turkey is being sought by Premier Venizelos of Greece, The premier declares his government considers such a calamity most probable since the advent of Turkish minister of war. Enver Bey is It was learned here today in authoritative circles that this was the chief object of the visit of the Greek statesman to the various European capitals. It was added that Greece fears the result of such a conflict. as Turkey is indisputably stronger on the sea since her purchase of the Brazilian dreadnaught Rio de Janeiro, In the course of his conferences alta the European statesmen, Premier Vin- izelos is endeavoring to bte ponement of the evacuation by the Greek troops of that portion of man of the Albanian frontier and the permanent acquisition by Greece of i ost of the Aegian islands,

headlines.title: HEARINGS OVER STEEL DISSOLUTION RESUMED
headlines.subtitle: Beaumont Mam Testifies His Company Bought From Corporation at Higher Prices.
headlines.fullText: NEW ORLEANS, Jan. 11 .- Hearings in the government's dissolution suit against the United States Steel Corporation were resumed here today before J. A. Brown, special examiner for the department of Justice. The entire morning session was taken up with the testimony of R. G. Wilder of the Norwell-Wilder Company of Beaumont, Texas, The subs that his company frequently made purchases of steel from Mississippi. Alabama and Texas are to testify before the hearings are concluded here.

headlines.title: WEST SEVENTH BRIDGE MAY BE OPENED SUNDAY
headlines.subtitle: Delayed Brick Arrives and Contractors Will Begin Paving Structure Immediately.
headlines.fullText: The West Seventh street bridge will be ready for travel perhane by Sunday and almost certainly by the middle of next week. headlines.title: INDICTMENT RUMORS REVIVE MOYER CASE
headlines.subtitle: Two Concerned in Deportation Said to Have Been Accused-Union-
headlines.fullText: HOUGHTON, Jan. 14 .- Interest in the recent deportation from the string zone of Charles H. Moyer, president of the Western Federation of Miners, was renewed here today. "It was rumored the special grand Jury which has been investigating the copper strike. had returned indret- two men who are said to have assisted in forcing Moyer to leave Hancock. Reports also were In circulation that several true bille against members of the Western Federation of Miners had been returned.
